meta.title: Is 36.4°C Normal? Body Temperature Chart & Celsius to Fahrenheit Guide
meta.description: Is 36.4°C a normal body temperature? See a complete body temperature chart by age, learn how to convert 36.4 Celsius to Fahrenheit (97.52°F), and when to see a doctor.

bodyTempRanges.title: 36.4°C: Modern Research Updates Normal Body Temperature
bodyTempRanges.subtitle: Why 36.4°C Matters in 2025
bodyTempRanges.intro: A body temperature of 36.4°C (97.52°F) is considered completely normal and healthy for most people. Normal body temperature varies by age — infants typically range from 36.5°C to 37.5°C, children from 36.0°C to 37.2°C, and adults from 36.1°C to 37.2°C. A fever is generally defined as a temperature of 38°C (100.4°F) or higher. Use the calculator below to convert 36.4°C to Fahrenheit, and explore our complete body temperature chart by age group.
bodyTempRanges.normalHighlight:
✅ MODERN AVERAGE LOWER LIMIT
36.4°C = 97.5°F is the new normal range floor

researchUpdate.title: 📊 Stanford Research: Body Temperatures Are Dropping
researchUpdate.content: A 2020 Stanford Medicine study analyzed 677,000 temperature measurements over 157 years and found average body temperatures have decreased by 0.03°C per decade. The traditional 37°C (98.6°F) standard from 1868 is outdated. Modern normal range: 36.4°C to 36.6°C (97.5°F to 97.9°F).

measurementMethods.title: 36.4°C Readings: Updated Normal Standards by Method
measurementMethods.intro: 36.4°C (97.5°F) represents the new lower limit of normal. Understanding measurement differences is crucial as medical standards update from the old 37°C (98.6°F) benchmark.

newborn.title: 👶 Newborns - Modern Healthy Baseline
points: 36.4°C (97.5°F) aligns with new lower average standards
Newborns often run cooler than old 37°C benchmark
Monitor patterns - single readings matter less
Contact pediatrician only if consistently below 35.8°C

infant.title: 👶 Infants - Within Updated Normal Range

points: 36.4°C (97.5°F) is the new average lower limit
Stanford study: 0.03°C drop per decade since 1860s
Better health, lower metabolism, indoor living explain drop
Your 'low' temp is actually modern normal

temperatureScale.title: Updated Body Temperature Scale: Old vs New Standards

q1.question: Is 36.4°C (97.5°F) too low? Why am I below 98.6°F?
q1.answer: 36.4°C is NOT too low. Stanford Medicine's 2020 research shows average body temperatures have dropped 0.03°C per decade since the 1860s. The 37°C (98.6°F) 'standard' is outdated. Modern normal range is 36.4°C to 36.6°C (97.5°F to 97.9°F). Your temperature is perfectly healthy.

q2.question: Why have body temperatures dropped since the 1800s?
q2.answer: Stanford researchers identified three factors: 1) Better health and lower inflammation due to medical advances; 2) Reduced metabolic rate from modern sedentary lifestyles; 3) Climate-controlled environments reducing body workload. 36.4°C reflects these improvements, not a problem.

q3.question: What did the Stanford body temperature study find?
q3.answer: The 2020 Stanford study analyzed 677,423 temperature measurements from 1892-2017. Key findings: Men's temps dropped 0.59°C, women's 0.32°C. Average temps decreased 0.03°C per birth decade. The 37°C (98.6°F) standard from 1868 is no longer accurate for modern populations. 36.4°C-36.6°C is the new normal.

q4.question: Is 36.4°C normal for babies and children?
q4.answer: Yes. Children show the same temperature decline as adults in Stanford's research. 36.4°C (97.5°F) is a healthy temperature for all ages. Pediatricians now recognize broader normal ranges. Contact doctors only for consistent readings below 35.5°C or accompanying illness symptoms.

q5.question: Should I trust modern 36.4°C standards or old 37°C?
q5.answer: Trust modern research. The 37°C (98.6°F) standard comes from a single 1868 German study of 25,000 people. Modern Stanford research (2020) uses 677,000+ measurements. Medical institutions including Cleveland Clinic and Johns Hopkins now accept 36.4°C-36.6°C as the updated normal range. 36.4°C is medically sound.

q6.question: How do I convert 36.4°C to Fahrenheit correctly?
q6.answer: To convert 36.4°C to Fahrenheit: Multiply 36.4 by 9/5 (or 1.8) = 65.52; Add 32 to the result: 65.52 + 32 = 97.52; Rounded: 36.4°C = 97.5°F. This temperature represents the modern average lower limit according to 2020 Stanford Medical research.
